data.title: Old National outlines 4%-6% 2026 loan growth while keeping full-year guidance unchanged
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old National Bancorp (ONB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Earlier today, Old National reported first quarter 2026 earnings that exceeded our internal expectations and analyst estimates." (CEO & Chairman James Ryan)

  • "We delivered robust loan growth, powered by continued strength in our core deposit franchise and disciplined funding management in a highly competitive market." (CEO & Chairman Ryan)

  • "We also acted decisively on capital returns, repurchasing shares during the quarter, including reducing Otto Bremer Trust position in Old National, and we intend to deploy the remaining authorization over the course of the program." (CEO & Chairman Ryan)

  • "This is not a time where we need acquisitions to achieve our objectives." (CEO & Chairman Ryan)

  • "We reported GAAP 1Q earnings per share of $0.59." (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran)

  • "Excluding $0.02 of merger-related expenses and a noncash expense associated with the final distribution of a legacy First Midwest pension plan, adjusted earnings per share were $0.61." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Specifically, during the first quarter, we returned $151 million to shareholders." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Outlook

  • "Slide 16 includes our outlook for the full year 2026, which is unchanged from our prior guidance." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "We believe our current pipeline supports full year loan growth of 4% to 6% and based on the results of the first quarter, we suspect we may trend to the higher end of this range." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Our NII guidance remains unchanged, and our balance sheet remains neutrally positioned to short-term interest rates." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "In aggregate, you'll note that we expect full year results that yield 15% plus growth in earnings per share and again, feature positive operating leverage with peer-leading profitability, good growth in fees, controlled expenses and normalized credit." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Financial Results

  • "We reported GAAP 1Q earnings per share of $0.59." (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran)

  • "Total loans grew 8% annualized from the last quarter, led by 16.9% annualized growth in C&I." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Total deposits increased 4.2% annualized, primarily driven by commercial and retail growth and partially offset by seasonally lower public funds balances." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Adjusted noninterest income ... was $122 million for the quarter, exceeding our guidance." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Adjusted noninterest expense was $354 million for the quarter." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "We reported a record low 46% adjusted efficiency ratio, and we have now realized 100% of the $111 million of annual run rate cost saves that were anticipated with Bremer." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.: "what gives you confidence in the guide and what are the major puts and takes you see?" Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "we've got a more cooperative yield curve today than what we had on average for the first quarter" and "we've got $5.5 billion sitting in the pipeline, up 14% year-over-year."

  • Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi: "Why not be more aggressive on the shareholder buyback or return?" Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "we've got $383 million left on this existing authorization" and "We would fully intend to use that through the end of that authorization in February."

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: "Has anything changed environmentally in your favor?" Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "we're leaning into go-to-market strategies" and "We've seen that really come together nicely this quarter."

  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: "How do you view the interplay" between CET1 and TCE? CEO & Chairman Ryan: "we're just trying to figure out what's the right long-term view and not get caught up in today's whatever short-term window might be."

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: "what you're doing on the AI front"? CEO & Chairman Ryan: "We've got an AI center of excellence stood up" and "Probably the first 1 for us that we're going to dive deeper into is in risk management."

  • Jon Rau (for Jared Shaw), Barclays: leadership changes and hiring focus? Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "Primarily on the C&I side, when you think about asset-based lending and core C&I middle market banking."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral on net interest income visibility and capital return pace, pressing on the path to the NII guide and buybacks, including "the year started a little weaker than at least the market had expected" (Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.) and "Why not be more aggressive on the shareholder buyback or return?" (Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly using stabilizing language on margin and guidance, including "stable to improving is the right way to think about it" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran) and "nothing we are seeing changes our outlook" (CEO & Chairman Ryan).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language stayed focused on organic growth and self-investment, while the Q1 discussion added more emphasis on proposed capital rule changes and related optionality, including "we would clearly expect a capital benefit under the proposed capital rule changes" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 reiterated the strategic stance against near-term deals more forcefully, with "This is not a time where we need acquisitions to achieve our objectives" (CEO & Chairman James Ryan), compared with Q4: "it's not a focus" for M&A (CEO & Chairman Ryan).

  • Capital return discussion evolved from Q4: "we plan to be more active than we were last year" (CEO & Chairman Ryan) to Q1’s clearer execution and remaining capacity, including "We repurchased 3.9 million shares during the current quarter" and "have $383 million remaining under our program" (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran).

  • Guidance posture was consistent, with Q1: "unchanged from our prior guidance" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran) echoing Q4’s framing of full-year 2026 expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The quarter brought higher for longer rate outlook and continued industry uncertainty." (CEO & Chairman James Ryan)

  • "The deposit competition is intense, but rational." (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran)

  • "Criticized and classified loans increased $113 million this quarter as Bremer loans transitioned to Old National's asset quality framework." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Given the continued focus on loans to non-depository financial institutions, we'd again like to emphasize that our exposure is de minimis." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a start that "reinforces our confidence in the full year plan" (CEO & Chairman James Ryan), while keeping full-year guidance intact and pointing to record pipelines and disciplined deposit pricing as key supports for "stable to improving" net interest income and margin (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran). Executives emphasized continued capital return through the existing buyback authorization, reiterated that acquisitions are not needed to hit objectives, and highlighted potential additional capital flexibility if proposed capital rules are finalized.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sportradar falls amid Muddy Waters short call (update)
data.description:
Update 3:05pm: Adds company response.

Sportradar (SRAD) plunged 23% amid a short report from Muddy Waters Research. 

Muddy Waters disclosed that it's short shares of Sportradar. The Muddy Waters report came after a short report from Callisto Research on Wednesday.

Sportradar responded to the short seller reports in a statement emailed to Seeking Alpha.

 "A short report issued today contains factual inaccuracies about the company, and we unequivocally challenge these assertions.

The report demonstrates a fundamental misunderstanding of our business and the industry and was authored by a short seller trying to erode shareholder value and profit from stock disruption.

Sportradar works exclusively with licensed operators, follows strict global compliance, and due diligence standards, and we stand by our independently audited financial statements, risk disclosures, and information provided to investors and regulators," Sportradar said in the statement.

Sportradar (SRAD) has a short interest of 4.9%.

Sportradar (SRAD) is set to release its Q1 results on May 6.

data.title: Teleflex gains on report PE firms teamed up for takeover offer (update)
data.description:
Update 2:53pm: Adds detail on offer price.

Teleflex (TFX) rose 9.7% after a report that CVC Capital Partners and GTCR  teamed up for a takeover offer for the medical equipment provider. 

The PE firms have been working together to consider a possible takeover for Teleflex (TFX), according to a  Bloomberg report late Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

One of the offers, possibly from the CVC and GTCR consortium, may have been for around $170 a share, according to traders, who cited a Betaville item circulating on Wednesday. 

It wasn't clear if Teleflex would be receptive to a takeout and there's no guarantee that an approach will lead to a transaction, according to the report. 

CVC, GTCR, and Teleflex declined to comment to Bloomberg.

A potential offer comes as activist investor Irenic Capital Management last month sent a letter to the medical device maker urging the company to explore strategic alternatives, including a potential buyout, and revamp its board.

data.title: Albemarle downgraded at Rothschild Redburn on lithium pricing lag
data.description:
Albemarle (ALB) down 2.2% in Wednesday's trading as Rothschild Redburn downgraded the lithium miner to Neutral from Buy with a $188 price target, saying a return to market surplus in 2027 will weigh on prices and erode the investment case for major producers.

Rothschild analyst Mazahir Mammadli said he expects a rollercoaster trajectory in Albemarle's (ALB) earnings due to spodumene pricing and joint venture income dynamics, and noted that pricing hits earnings with a three-month lag, meaning Albemarle's pricing gain likely will peak in Q3 if spot prices peak in Q2.

Central to Mammadli's bear case is a dynamic playing out in China, where export tax rebates for batteries and battery components are being phased out from 2027, and the analyst said the industry is responding with elevated production in 2026, pulling forward lithium demand, but argued that "a reversal of this overproduction next year will lead to lithium demand deceleration."

With supply ramping up across China, Argentina, and the U.S., the analyst said he expects lithium carbonate equivalent prices to fall to $15-$16/kg by year-end.

data.title: United Airlines cuts 2026 forecast on fuel costs, ranks only fifth among U.S. airlines by Quant
data.description:
United Airlines Holdings (UAL) traded lower after cutting its profit outlook amid the recent surge in jet fuel prices.

Despite being the carrier most in focus this earnings cycle, UAL ranks only fifth among U.S. passenger airline stocks on Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, sitting behind not just the two Buy-rated names, but also rivals Delta (DAL) and JetBlue (JBLU), each of which edges it out by a thin margin.

With a Hold rating of 3.09, UAL's quant profile reflects the tension between its strong operational execution and the overhang of elevated fuel costs and compressed forward earnings estimates.

Topping the Quant rankings are American Airlines (AAL) and Southwest Airlines (LUV), the only two carriers currently receiving bullish assessments with scores of 4.31 and 4.12, respectively. Delta (DAL) and JetBlue (JBLU) trail with Hold ratings of 3.12 and 3.10, just ahead of UAL's 3.09. SkyWest (SKYW) and Alaska Air Group (ALK) round out the list with scores of 2.95 and 2.90, edging closer to bearish territory.

Seeking Alpha's Quant system grades stocks based on valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability on a scale of 1 to 5. Scores above 3.5 indicate a bullish rating; 2.5 or below signals a bearish profile.

Here is the list:

  1. American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL), Quant Rating: 4.31

  2. Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV), Quant Rating: 4.12

  3. Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), Quant Rating: 3.12

  4. JetBlue Airways Corporation (JBLU), Quant Rating: 3.10

  5. United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL), Quant Rating: 3.09

  6. SkyWest, Inc. (SKYW), Quant Rating: 2.95

  7. Alaska Air Group, Inc. (ALK), Quant Rating: 2.90

data.title: Wells Fargo lent to MFS as Barclays pulled out - report
data.description:
Wells Fargo (WFC) entered into a loan agreement with a Market Financial Solutions-associated property portfolio days after UK bank Barclays (BCS) started freezing accounts of the property lender that was on the brink of collapse, according to a media report on Wednesday.

A company linked to MFS borrowed £143M ($193M) from Wells Fargo in late November, Bloomberg News reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter and documents that its reporters viewed. Barclays (BCS), meanwhile, was repaid about the same amount, they said.

The new loan closed soon after Barclays began to freeze some of MFS's accounts, putting the property lender on a path for its eventual collapse, Bloomberg said. The company failed in February amid allegations of widespread wrongdoing.

Wells Fargo's (WFC)  £143 debt is among the more than £2B of loans tied to MFS. Creditors have alleged a £1.3B gap in its finances, and the UK's Financial Conduct Authority is investigating MFS's operations.

Even with the payment it received soon after the Wells Fargo transaction closed, Barclays (BCS) remains MFS's biggest lender. It is still owed ~£500M, Bloomberg said. Barclays CEO CS Venkatakrishnan expects its impairment for the MFS exposure will be "materially lower" than that amount, he said last month.

Meanwhile, Wells Fargo (WFC) added $174M of charge-offs in its commercial and industrial loans for Q1 due to a "single fraud-related loss" in the real estate finance category, Chief Financial Officer Mike Santomassimo said in the bank's earnings call earlier this month. He didn't name the company involved but described it as an "isolated event."

Others that were left holding MFS-related debt are Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) and  Apollo Global Management's (APO) Atlas SP unit.

data.title: Bell signs agreements for three Bell 429 helicopters in U.K., Estonia
data.description:
Bell Textron said it has signed purchase agreements for three Bell 429 helicopters with VIP operators in the United Kingdom and Estonia, adding to the aircraft’s presence in the European market.

The unit of Textron (TXT) didn't disclose the value of the agreements or identify the buyers.

 

 

The Bell 429 is a light twin-engine helicopter commonly used for corporate transport, emergency medical services, law enforcement and utility missions. Bell said demand in Europe has remained steady for corporate-configured aircraft, particularly among private and executive transport operators.

According to the company, more than 500 Bell 429 helicopters are in service globally, with the fleet logging more than 735,000 flight hours.

The aircraft is configured to seat up to seven passengers and is equipped for instrument flight rules, allowing operations in a wider range of weather conditions than aircraft limited to visual flight rules. Bell also markets the model on the basis of cabin size and interior flexibility.

Last month, Bell announced additional interior options for the Bell 429 as part of its Designer Series package, aimed at customers seeking upgraded cabin finishes and layouts.

The latest agreements come as helicopter manufacturers continue to compete for private transport and charter demand in Europe, where operators often prioritize aircraft with executive interiors, twin-engine redundancy and all-weather capability.

data.title: GE's Colibrium Additive wins $31M Navy contract for metal 3D printing support
data.description:
Colibrium Additive, a GE Aerospace (GE) company, on Wednesday said it received a $31 million contract from the U.S. Naval Air Systems Command, or NAVAIR, to support an initiative aimed at speeding the testing, qualification and certification of metal parts made through additive manufacturing.

 

The program is intended to improve operational readiness by helping the Navy qualify replacement and new components more quickly, while reducing dependence on traditional supply chains for critical aviation parts.

 

Under the agreement, Colibrium Additive will provide six metal alloy Material Process Combinations, or MPCs, which include physical and mechanical property data used to qualify materials and manufacturing methods. The company will also optimize process parameters, consolidate specifications and establish design allowables for tested properties.

The scope includes expanding existing data packages for AlSi7Mg and IN718 alloys, while adding new packages for 17-4PH and 7050-RAM2. Those materials will join an existing portfolio that includes 316L stainless steel, cobalt-chrome and Ti64 titanium alloy.

The contract also calls for thin-wall fatigue characterization work to assess the durability and service life of lightweight geometries often used in aerospace applications. That testing is intended to support qualification of additively manufactured structural components for aviation use.

To support the effort, Colibrium Additive will supply three M Line metal 3D printing systems and one M2 Series 5 printer. The company will also provide engineering and support services that include material characterization data, manufacturing instructions and selected specifications for Navy components.

In addition, the program includes training for personnel involved in manufacturing, quality assurance, design, materials engineering and machine operation.

The Navy and other branches of the U.S. military have increasingly explored additive manufacturing as a way to shorten lead times for spare parts, sustain aging fleets and produce components closer to the point of need.

data.title: Antero Midstream, DT Midstream raised at Morgan Stanley; Targa top midstream pick
data.description:
Antero Midstream (AM) up 2.7% and DT Midstream (DTM) up 0.9% in Wednesday's trading as Morgan Stanley upgraded both names to Equal Weight from Underweight with respective $26 and $165 price targets, while raising WaterBridge International (WBI) to Overweight from Equal Weight with a $34 PT.

Antero Midstream (AM) is entering a multi-year growth phase driven by Antero Resources' (AR) expanding production and capital activity with potential upside from execution on growth, data center-related demand, and increased capital returns without significant capex growth, Morgan Stanley analyst Robert Kad said, adding his unlevered DCF-derived fair value of $26 implies 26.4% one-year total return upside, including a 4.2% dividend yield.

DT Midstream (DTM) has demonstrated stronger-than-expected growth origination, particularlyon its Midwest pipeline footprint, positioning it to benefit from data center and LNG demand, Kad wrote, and an expanding backlog supports accelerated EBITDA growth above its 5%-7% long-term target; the analyst's unlevered DCF-derived fair value of $165 implies 25.5% one-year total return upside, including a 2.6% dividend yield.

WaterBridge (WBI) offers the strongest EBITDA growth in Morgan Stanley's midstream coverage, Kad said, driven by advantaged Delaware Basin exposure and a leading water infrastructure position, with additional upside from a structural re-rating as water handling shifts from a services model toward midstream G&P valuation multiples; unlevered DCF-derived fair value of $34 implies 33.4% one-year total return upside, including a 1% dividend yield.

Additionally, Kad moved Targa Resources (TRGP) to his Top Pick within the midstream energy infrastructure sector, expecting Permian Basin-associated gas production to accelerate above market expectations following the addition of new pipeline takeaway projects in H2 2026.

data.title: TE Connectivity expects Q3 sales of $5B and adjusted EPS of around $2.83 as AI revenue outlook rises by $150M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TE Connectivity (TEL) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "Our second quarter sales were over $4.7 billion" and "we delivered record adjusted earnings per share of $2.73" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • "In the second quarter, we had record orders of over $5 billion" and "we announced that our Board approved a 10% increase to our quarterly cash dividend" (CEO Curtin).

  • "Orders were $5.3 billion with a book-to-bill of 1.12" and "over 70% of the company's order growth was in the Industrial segment" (CEO Curtin).

  • "In our digital data networks, we had another outstanding quarter" and "we acquired a leading technology for passive optical connectivity solutions" (CEO Curtin).

  • "We now expect our AI revenues in fiscal 2026 to be about $150 million higher than our view 90 days ago" and "this entire increase will be in the second half of the year" (CEO Curtin).

  • "For the quarter, we achieved adjusted operating income of over $1 billion and adjusted operating margins of 21.7%" (CFO Heath Mitts).

Outlook

  • "As we look to the third quarter, we are expecting third quarter sales to be $5 billion" and "we expect adjusted earnings per share to be up 17% year-over-year to around $2.83" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • "We expect Q3 [the adjusted effective tax rate] to be around 23% and the full year tax rate to be approximately 22%" (CFO Heath Mitts).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s outlook language, management shifted from guiding Q2 sales of $4.7 billion and adjusted EPS of around $2.65 to guiding Q3 sales of $5 billion and adjusted EPS of around $2.83 (CEO Curtin).

Financial results

  • "Our second quarter sales were over $4.7 billion" and "sales grew 15% on a reported basis and 7% organically year-over-year" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • "We delivered record adjusted earnings per share of $2.73" and "our operating margins on an adjusted basis were 22%" (CEO Curtin).

  • "GAAP operating income was $954 million" and it "included $8 million of acquisition-related charges, $10 million of restructuring and other charges and $57 million of amortization expense" (CFO Heath Mitts).

  • "GAAP EPS was $2.90 for the quarter" and it "included a $0.39 tax benefit primarily related to a settlement of prior period tax matters" (CFO Mitts).

  • "Cash from operations was $947 million and free cash flow was $680 million" and "through the first half of the fiscal year, free cash flow was a record $1.3 billion" (CFO Mitts).

Q&A

  • Scott Davis, Melius Research LLC: Can you talk about the $150 million bump up and when it ships? CEO Curtin: "the $150 million that I mentioned... relate to the second half" and "it will ramp in the second half."

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Can record orders be sustained and what did TE see in April amid geopolitical volatility? CEO Curtin: "in the first month since quarter end, the order momentum continues to be very strong" and "we have not seen any demand negative impacts."

  • Luke Junk, Baird: Updated view on copper vs. optical in AI and the RAM Photonics deal? CEO Curtin: "it's not copper or optical, it's copper and optical" and the acquisition "enables advancements in high-density fiber array connections."

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: What’s driving energy growth and commercial transportation strength? CEO Curtin: "probably about 60% or 2/3 of what we do is around utility and grid hardening" and commercial transportation is driven by "continued share gains from new program wins and increasing content per vehicle."

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA: What drives expected acceleration in auto content growth, and what’s the sequential DDN order trend? CEO Curtin: "we expect there to be 88 million to 89 million units" and on sequential DDN orders, "we can follow up later to your question. I just don't have it here in front of me."

  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: Portfolio actions, sensors, and buybacks vs. M&A? CEO Curtin: "we really like the portfolio" and it is "more about playing offense." CFO Mitts: "the pipeline is actually for M&A, it's actually -- it's pretty active right now" and "stay tuned."

  • Joseph Spak, UBS: How do inflation pressures affect margins next quarter? CFO Mitts: "we do have a playbook" and "there is a little bit of noise in our absolute margins for sure on that."

  • Joseph Giordano, TD Cowen: Is there a lag between pricing in orders and the P&L, and any update on CapEx? CFO Mitts: "we have taken our CapEx up this year" and "you should expect CapEx this year to be to run about 6% of revenue."

  • Guy Hardwick, Barclays: Are enterprise/telecom IT budgets being squeezed by AI spending? CEO Curtin: "we are seeing nice growth" in enterprise, telecom and wireless, though "it's not at the rates like I talked about 60%."

  • Asiya Merchant, Citigroup: Any supply tightness in CPUs/GPUs/memory affecting visibility and ramps? CEO Curtin: "we don't buy that" and "we're not seeing any slowdowns... If anything... they're increasing their ramps."

  • Steven Fox, Fox Advisors: How durable is energy growth over coming quarters? CEO Curtin: "with what's happening in utility, grid hardening and the industrial/data center space, it's full steam ahead" while "the biggest lumpiness" is "in the clean energy space."

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: Is optical additive to the AI content opportunity over time? CEO Curtin: "this will help us in the scale-out element" and "it's an increase of content that we have."

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: Downside if customers switch from copper to fiber? CEO Curtin: "it's not going to be an or, it's going to be an and" and "our TAM and copper is going to continue to grow due to the power elements that you need."

  • William Stein, Truist: Why has DDN revenue been flat sequentially despite bookings, and why did Industrial margins dip sequentially? CEO Curtin: "we're going to be stepping up here in the second half" and "the $3 billion... continues to shift towards the left." CFO Mitts: "you're going to have -- always have a little bit of noise in any quarter" and referenced "ramped some investments in our DDN business."

  • Shreyas Patil, Wolfe Research: How big is optical for AI today and where else bolster via M&A? CEO Curtin: "we're going to stay in passives" and "we will continue to look at via partnerships versus our organic development."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive overall, with persistent probing on AI durability, copper-vs.-optical transition risk, and margin sensitivity; one exchange pressed for downside framing (Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: "any way to frame the downside or the content loss").

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to backlog and orders; CEO Curtin said, "we expect our strong performance will continue," and emphasized, "it's not copper or optical, it's copper and optical."

  • Versus last quarter, the Q&A remained AI-centric, but shifted more toward optical roadmap specifics and capital deployment (M&A pipeline and CapEx), with a clearer instance of a deferred detail (CEO Curtin: "we can follow up later").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management raised its fiscal 2026 AI revenue outlook again, but the incremental uplift was smaller than the prior quarter’s update: Q2 call "about $150 million higher" vs. Q1 call "$200 million more" than the prior view 90 days earlier (CEO Terrence Curtin).

  • Industrial growth messaging stayed centered on AI and energy, while the Q2 call added a specific optical-related acquisition update: "we acquired a leading technology for passive optical connectivity solutions" (CEO Curtin).

  • Analysts stayed focused on reconciling bookings to revenue, but Q2 featured more direct scrutiny around optical transition downside and sequential margin movement; management leaned on long-term framing (CFO Mitts: "We tend to look at margins on a year-to-date or rolling basis").

Risks and concerns

  • "We continue to operate in a dynamic environment" and management cited "increased inflationary pressures across certain input costs such as oil-based resins and freight charges" tied to "higher energy costs and broader geopolitical tensions" (CFO Heath Mitts).

  • Mitigation actions were described as "optimization of our factory footprint, targeted pricing actions and ongoing productivity initiatives" and a "localization strategy around supply chain" (CFO Mitts).

  • For visibility and timing risk, management repeated that AI-related orders and ramps can be uneven: "there will be some lumpiness" (CEO Terrence Curtin).

Final takeaway

Management emphasized that Q2 combined record adjusted EPS of $2.73 with record orders of $5.3 billion, while raising its fiscal 2026 AI revenue outlook by $150 million, all of which it tied to second-half ramps and backlog extending into 2027. For Q3, management guided to $5 billion of sales and adjusted EPS of around $2.83, while also flagging a more dynamic cost backdrop (resins and freight) that it said it is managing through pricing, footprint actions, and productivity levers, alongside an AI-driven CapEx level of about 6% of revenue.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
